paragraphs.body: In the report, “JonBenet Ramsey Crime Museum”, Susan Frese observes and reports the missing person case of JonBenet Ramsey and writes a detailed documentary of a potential homicide that lingers within as well as the execution. She displays her point by developing several important ideas. First, Frese introduces what started the whole mystery murder, she recaps how John and Patsy Ramsey were greeted with a ransom note early in the morning demanding $118,000 for the safe return of their child. Next, she criticizes how unprofessional and unskilled the investigators were when they initiated the search; instead of locking the crime scene, they let everyone roam inside and destroy all potential evidence. Finally, Frese addresses that the JonBenet Ramsey case is ongoing and has been on and off, but potential suspects linger within the Ramsey home.

paragraphs.body: My whole life I’ve always thought doctors, police officers, and even dancers were perfect. Every time perfection was brought to topic, I thought of my brother who exceeds in everything he does. With years of knowledge and experience, I’ve always trusted them to perform with high expectations and flawlessness. In high-stakes situations shouldn’t they give them their all? However, I came to an understanding that they’re just humans, they’re not robots that can remember every single detail and manipulate each task with extreme precision and perfection. In reality, not all doctors come out saying the surgery was successful, not all detectives come back saying they finally solved the case, and not every military man comes back from war flawless with no scrapes on their skin. This is what makes us human.

annotations.context_left: that can remember every single detail and manipulate each task with extreme precision and

paragraphs.body: Ever since my childhood my parents and grandparents had high expectations for me. They were always seeking perfection out of me, knowing that I was the girl in the family, I was the one who would bring them success. Especially my grandparents, who were so influenced by Chinese culture, since they were from Fuzhou, China. They carried a lot of that culture into how they raised me.

annotations.text: They were always seeking perfection out of me, knowing that I was the girl in the family, I was the one who would bring them success.
annotations.comment: Interesting. Does being a girl contribute to the central focusing idea of your essay?

annotations.text: Chinese culture, since they were from Fuzhou, China. They carried a lot of that culture into how they raised me.
annotations.comment: Is this an idea you should develop? Let's figure out what your essay's central focusing idea is, then we'll know. :)

paragraphs.body: To get a better understanding of how I was raised as a kid, you must understand what the educational system in China was like. In China, students go to school from as early as 7:30 am to around 6 pm, and they work tirelessly. When they get home, they are followed by more extensive studying and homework. It’s pretty much required that they spend at least three hours on homework every night, meaning they won’t be done till 9 pm. After that, it’s a quick shower, sleep, and then they wake up to start the whole routine all over again.

My parents made me follow this system starting as early as sixth grade, right after I finished elementary school. I was forced to go to prep school for three hours every single day after regular school ended, and that went on all the way until I became a senior. To be honest, I never knew what the word "freedom" even meant because I was so controlled by my parents. I had no time for myself. I would get home as late as 8 p.m., just to sleep and do it all over again the next day.

annotations.text: To be honest, I never knew what the word "freedom"

paragraphs.body: So, how does this tie into perfection? Well, after spending 7 years, 6 days a week, 7,665 hours, and 459,900 minutes studying for my future, I thought I had finally become the "perfect" version of myself that my parents always anticipated me to be. I studied for the specialized high school tests, the SHSAT, the ACT, and the SAT and everyone would think that with all that studying, I would’ve gotten close to a score of 1500 on the SAT, right? However, no, that was not the case, my score wasn’t anywhere near 1500. It was such a disappointment, especially after my parents had spent so much money on my education, hoping I’d be exactly what they wanted.

On top of that, they always compared me to my brother. Honestly, when it comes to perfection, I thought he was perfect. He never went to prep school like I did, but he scored higher on the SAT than me. He did things so effortlessly, he never studied, just played video games all day, and still got 100s on every test. Watching him get the praise I always wanted made me so mad. Why him? Why not me?

I never really thought about why I avoided him or why I gave him attitude every time he tried to talk to me. I stayed distant from him for as long as I can remember. But then, when the day came that he left for college, something strange happened. I found myself alone in my room, feeling suffocated. Tears just started rolling down my face, and I remember thinking, “Do I miss him?”

The feeling of hating him never really went away, until the day my parents sat me down. They told me my brother had gotten accepted to Cornell University, my dream school, the one I always wanted to go to but knew I couldn’t. That moment made me feel even more infuriated. My brother, the perfect one, got accepted into the school I could only dream about. Of course, every college wanted him. But then my mom said, “Your brother didn’t go.” Her words hit me hard. He didn’t go. Why would he pass up an opportunity like that? The reason was so dumb, that I laughed. My brother had forgotten to check his email for the acceptance letter, and since he didn’t respond in time, Cornell rejected him. He ended up getting dropped from every school except for one safety school which was Arizona University.

annotations.text: The feeling of hating him never really went away, until the day my parents sat me down. They told me my brother had gotten accepted to Cornell University, my dream school, the one I always wanted to go to but knew I couldn’t.
annotations.comment: Wow. What a revealing detail. Nice voice, too.

paragraphs.body: I was shocked. How could someone so “perfect” mess up such a huge opportunity to go to Cornell? That moment stuck with me. Seeing how disappointed my parents were and hearing them talk badly about him for the first time made me feel lighter, almost like I could finally breathe. Maybe he wasn’t so perfect after all. Maybe he’s just like me. I wouldn’t have messed up a chance like that, but that’s not the point. Everyone has flaws. His was being careless and not checking his emails, mine was not being able to be as smart as everyone else no matter how hard I tried. Nobody’s perfect, no matter how much you think someone is so well put together, trust me they’re not.

annotations.text: How could someone so “perfect” mess up such a huge opportunity to go to Cornell? That moment stuck with me. Seeing how disappointed my parents were and hearing them talk badly about him for the first time made me feel lighter, almost like I could finally breathe. Maybe he wasn’t so perfect after all. Maybe he’s just like me.
annotations.comment: Let's discuss what "perfection" means to you. This is an interesting moment.

paragraphs.body: In the murder case of JonBenet Ramsey, it’s been said that the investigators of the crime scene made a lot of crucial mistakes that resulted in the cold case of JonBenet. According to Susan Frese, she states, “Before JonBenét’s body was even found, there were many investigative mistakes made. Only JonBenét’s room was cornered off, so friends and family roamed the rest of the house, picking up things and potentially destroying evidence.” In other words, Frese believes that one main key factor that resulted in the mystery of JonBenet’s case has to do with the investigators screwing up the whole crime scene and not putting their skill and years of work into action. In my perspective I believe this is normal, not everybody is perfect, what makes us human are our flaws and imperfections. Although there were crucial mistakes made, even the wise people do the same.

paragraphs.body: When it comes to perfection, humans aren’t always the center of attention. There is no such thing as a flawless execution or an impeccable performance. According to Frese, she notes “DNA did not match the profile generated from the samples found”, “The profile from her underwear was entered into CODIS in 2003, but no matches have been identified”, and “this analysis was not further supported by any other evidence”. This only shows the many imperfections of the evidence collected. When investigators thought they finally concluded the case, uncertainty and denial reemerged.

No matter how much effort is put into something, perfection is not something anyone can achieve. Whether it’s me performing academically, or a crime investigator performing professionally, we will never oversee what true flawlessness is, no one will. Not every score is going to be aced the way I want it to be, and not every crime case is going to be solved the way investigators want it to be, it’s just the way of life as humans.
